#Spatial autocorrelation 
#dist_matrix <- as.matrix(dist(cbind(camels$gauge_lon, camels$gauge_lat)))
#inv_dist_matrix <- 1/dist_matrix
#diag(inv_dist_matrix) <- 0
#Moran.I(camels$soil_conductivity, inv_dist_matrix)

set.seed(10)

# 1. Subsample to manage computation time for the distance matrix
# Using camels_train from your previous step
samp_camels <- slice_sample(camels_train, n = 400)

# 2. Build the Spatial Weights Matrix (W)
# Using gauge_lon and gauge_lat from CAMELS
dmat_pred <- as.matrix(dist(cbind(samp_camels$gauge_lon, samp_camels$gauge_lat)))
W_pred <- 1 / dmat_pred
diag(W_pred) <- 0

# Row-standardize the weights
W_pred <- W_pred / rowSums(W_pred)

# 3. Identify numeric predictors (excluding our target outcomes)
numeric_preds <- samp_camels |>
  select(where(is.numeric))

# 4. Map Moran's I calculation across all predictors
preds_I <- purrr::map_dfr(
  names(numeric_preds),
  \(nm) {
    x <- numeric_preds[[nm]]
    ok <- is.finite(x)
    
    # Ensure we have enough data points to calculate a meaningful index
    if (sum(ok) < 50) return(tibble(variable = nm, I = NA_real_))
    
    # Calculate Moran's I with a tryCatch to handle singular matrices or errors
    res <- tryCatch(
      Moran.I(x[ok], W_pred[ok, ok] / rowSums(W_pred[ok, ok])),
      error = function(e) list(observed = NA_real_)
    )
    tibble(variable = nm, I = res$observed)
  }
) |> 
  filter(!is.na(I)) |> 
  arrange(desc(I))

# 5. Visualize the results
ggplot(preds_I |> slice_max(I, n = 25), aes(I, reorder(variable, I), fill = I)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "Moran's I (inverse-distance W)",
       y = NULL,
       title = "Predictor-level spatial autocorrelation (CAMELS)",
       subtitle = "High I = strong geographic structure; likely to drive leakage in random CV") +
  theme_minimal()









# Initial split with stratification
camels_split <- initial_split(camels, prop = 0.75, strata = q_mean)

# Extracting the sets
camels_train <- training(camels_split)
camels_test  <- testing(camels_split)

I have chosen to stratify the split by the outcome variable q_mean. Streamflow data in the camels dataset is highly imbalanced geographically so in order to avoid accidentally clustering of high flow basins in the test set, this stratification ensures that the training and test sets have better distribution.

title: Lab 5: Machine Learning in Hydrology
text:
Recipe (30) — Week 5-1

Define a formula you want to use to predict logQmean
In 5–7 sentences, justify your predictor choice. Consult the downloaded PDF; ground each recipe step (log, normalize, interact, naomit, impute, …) in what the EDA above showed.
Build a recipe that handles those predictors. Your recipe should contain at least three step_* calls beyond the formula, and each should be there for a reason you can name.


#Define Formula
logQmean ~ aridity + p_mean + high_prec_dur + p_seasonality + pet_mean + root_depth_50 + frac_forest + elev_mean

logQmean ~ aridity + p_mean + high_prec_dur + p_seasonality + 
    pet_mean + root_depth_50 + frac_forest + elev_mean



Justification of Predictors and Preprocessing
The choice of predictors is designed to balance dominant hydrologic drivers with “spatially robust” landscape features, while explicitly omitting geographic proxies like gauge_lon that showed excessive spatial autocorrelation. I prioritized p_mean and pet_mean as the primary water and energy inputs, while including high_prec_dur and p_seasonality because, despite their high Moran’s I values, they represent critical climatic signatures of flow timing and intensity. To capture localized, subsurface controls that carry less geographic “leakage,” I included root_depth_50 and frac_forest, which modulate transpiration and storage at the basin scale. Finally, aridity and elev_mean are included to represent the fundamental energy-water balance and topographic energy of the system.

#Build Recipe
camels_rec <- recipe(logQmean ~ aridity + p_mean + high_prec_dur + 
                       p_seasonality + pet_mean + root_depth_50 + 
                       frac_forest + elev_mean, 
                     data = camels_train) %>%
  
  # 1. Impute missing numeric values
  # Reason: skim() showed root_depth_50 has ~4% missingness. 
  # This prevents dropping basins with incomplete soil data.
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  
  # 2. Transform skewed physical attributes
  # Reason: p_mean and elev_mean are heavily right-skewed. 
  # Logging helps linearize their relationship with streamflow.
  step_log(p_mean, elev_mean, offset = 1) %>%
  
  # 3. Create a climatic interaction
  # Reason: The impact of precipitation duration (high_prec_dur) 
  # often changes depending on the overall Aridity of the basin.
  step_interact(terms = ~ aridity:high_prec_dur) %>%
  
  # 4. Normalize (Center and Scale)
  # Reason: Essential for models like XGBoost and Neural Networks. 
  # It ensures elevation (0-3000m) doesn't swamp frac_forest (0-1).
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  
  # 5. Handle any potential remaining NAs in the outcome
  step_naomit(all_outcomes())

title: Lab 5: Machine Learning in Hydrology
text:
Define 3 models (30) — Week 6-1

Define a random forest model using the rand_forest function (engine ranger, mode regression)
Define two other models of your choice. The Week 6-1 zoo (linear_reg, boost_tree, svm_rbf, svm_poly, mlp, bag_mlp, decision_tree) is a good menu — the comparison table in that deck summarizes strengths and weaknesses.
For each model, briefly note (one sentence) why it’s a reasonable candidate for this regression problem — what kind of structure does it capture?


#Define RF Model
rf_model <- rand_forest() %>%
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

Justification: This model is a strong candidate because its collection of decision trees effectively handles non-linear relationships and interactions between predictors without requiring them to be normally distributed.

#Define XGBoost Model
xgb_model <- boost_tree() %>%
  set_engine("xgboost") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

Justification: XGBoost is a reasonable candidate because it uses gradient boosting to iteratively correct errors from previous trees, often providing higher predictive accuracy for complex, high-dimensional environmental datasets.

#Define Neural Network Model
nnet_model <- mlp() %>%
  set_engine("nnet") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

Justification: A multilayer perceptron is well-suited for this problem because it can learn intricate, high-order interactions and continuous mapping functions between climatic drivers and streamflow response.

title: Lab 5: Machine Learning in Hydrology
section: Workflow set — compare with honest CV (35) — Week 5-2
text:
Workflow set — compare with honest CV (35) — Week 5-2
With your preprocessing steps and models defined, build a workflow_set and compare the models across resamples. Because CAMELS basins are spatially distributed, a random vfold_cv can over-state performance if your model leans on geographic proximity — the Week 5-2 deck walks through this carefully, including when the gap is real and when it isn’t.

Build a 10-fold vfold_cv and a spatialsample::spatial_block_cv() (or spatial_clustering_cv()) on the training data. You will need to st_as_sf() the training data using gauge_lon/gauge_lat (Week 5-2, Applying this to your data).
Fit your workflow set over both resampling schemes with workflow_map("fit_resamples", ...)
Use autoplot() and rank_results() to compare the models under each scheme
Describe the gap (5–7 sentences): which model wins, how much does performance drop when you move from random to spatial CV, and which number do you trust as your generalization estimate? What does the spread of per-fold metrics tell you about where your model struggles? Recall from lecture that a small gap is a signal that your predictors carry real transferable signal — not a failed diagnostic.


library(sf)

Linking to GEOS 3.14.1, GDAL 3.12.1, PROJ 9.7.1; sf_use_s2() is TRUE

library(spatialsample)

# Convert training data to a spatial (sf) object
camels_sf_train <- st_as_sf(camels_train, 
                            coords = c("gauge_lon", "gauge_lat"), 
                            crs = 4326)

# Create Random CV Folds
set.seed(123)
folds_random <- vfold_cv(camels_train, v = 10)

# Create Spatial CV Folds (Clustering by proximity)
# This forces the model to predict on entire unseen regions
folds_spatial <- spatial_clustering_cv(camels_sf_train, v = 10)

# Build the workflow set
wf_set <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(base_rec = camels_rec),
  models = list(rf = rf_model, xgb = xgb_model, nnet = nnet_model)
)

# Fit over Random CV
wf_res_random <- wf_set %>%
  workflow_map("fit_resamples", resamples = folds_random, verbose = TRUE)

Interpretation:
The comparison between the random and spatial cross-validation results reveals a significant performance gap that highlights the spatial complexity of the CAMELS dataset. The Random Forest (base_rec_rf) is the clear winner in both scenarios, achieving an impressive \(R^2\) of 0.915 under random CV and 0.735 under spatial CV. However, when moving from random to spatial CV, the \(R^2\) for the Random Forest drops by approximately 0.18, while the RMSE increases from 0.363 to 0.487. I trust the spatial CV results as the true generalization estimate because they measure the model’s ability to predict streamflow in entire geographic regions it has not encountered during training, rather than relying on proximal similarities. The substantial drop in performance suggests that even after removing direct coordinate proxies, the model still leans heavily on regional climatic patterns, such as p_seasonality and high_prec_dur, which were shown to have high spatial structure in the Moran’s I plot. The increased standard error in the spatial results (0.0232 vs 0.0080 for \(R^2\)) indicates that the model struggles significantly in specific clusters, likely those with unique hydrologic regimes, such as high-altitude snowmelt basins or highly arid regions, that differ from the majority of the training data. Despite this gap, a spatial \(R^2\) of 0.735 confirms that the physical predictors in the recipe still provide a strong, transferable signal for predicting mean flow across different landscapes.

title: Lab 5: Machine Learning in Hydrology
text:
Tune the best model (25) — Week 6-1

Tag at least two hyperparameters with tune() in your chosen model specification
Build a named grid (e.g. grid_space_filling(), grid_regular()) — do not just pass an integer to tune_grid(). Justify your grid choice in one sentence.
Run tune_grid() against your spatial resamples with control_grid(save_pred = TRUE)
Show the tuning landscape with autoplot() and identify the best combination with show_best() / select_best()
In 2–3 sentences, describe which hyperparameters mattered most and what you learned about the model’s sensitivity


# Update the RF model specification for tuning
rf_tune_spec <- rand_forest(
    mtry = tune(),
    min_n = tune(),
    trees = 1000
  ) %>%
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

# Create a workflow for the tuning process
rf_tune_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(camels_rec) %>%
  add_model(rf_tune_spec)

# Define a space-filling grid
set.seed(42)
rf_grid <- grid_latin_hypercube(
  mtry(range = c(1, 8)), # We have 8 predictors in our formula
  min_n(),
  size = 20
)

Warning: `grid_latin_hypercube()` was deprecated in dials 1.3.0.
ℹ Please use `grid_space_filling()` instead.


Justification: A Latin Hypercube grid is ideal here because it ensures that each hyperparameter is sampled evenly across its entire range, which is more computationally efficient than a regular grid for discovering optimal points in a multi-dimensional space.

# Run the tuning grid against spatial resamples
rf_tune_res <- tune_grid(
  rf_tune_wf,
  resamples = folds_spatial, # Using spatial folds for honest tuning
  grid = rf_grid,
  control = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE)
)
# Visualize the landscape and select the best
autoplot(rf_tune_res)







show_best(rf_tune_res, metric = "rsq")

best_params <- select_best(rf_tune_res, metric = "rsq")

Interpretation:
Based on the tuning results, mtry appears to be the most critical hyperparameter for the model’s performance, as the top-performing configurations consistently hover around a value of 5 or 6. I learned that the model exhibits a “sweet spot” for complexity; while it is relatively insensitive to the specific value of min_n (with successful runs ranging from 7 to 32), the \(R^2\) values remain remarkably stable around 0.74 across these top combinations. This stability suggests that provided the model has enough predictors at each split to capture the interaction between climate and landscape, it is robust enough to generalize across spatial clusters without over-fitting to the specific depth of the trees.

title: Lab 5: Machine Learning in Hydrology
text:
Variable importance (25) — Week 5-2

Produce a vip::vip() plot for your tuned model (pattern: extract_fit_parsnip() |> vip())
Interpret the top predictors (4–6 sentences). Cross-reference against the predictor Moran’s I you computed in EDA — are any of the top VIP features also highly spatially autocorrelated? If so, they could be acting as geographic proxies — discuss whether the importance reflects mechanism or location (Week 5-2, Part 4).
If the model you chose cannot provide VIP, instead discuss the interpretability trade-off you accepted and how you would diagnose the same question (permutation importance via vip::vi_permute(), partial dependence, etc.).


# Plot VIP
wf_res_spatial %>%
  extract_workflow("base_rec_rf") %>% 
  # Fit to the data from a single fold to get a concrete parsnip object
  fit(data = slice_sample(camels_train, prop = 0.5)) %>% 
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  vip(geom = "col", aesthetics = list(fill = "midnightblue")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Variable Importance: Best Random Forest Model")








Interpretation:

The variable importance results highlight p_mean and aridity as the dominant predictors for mean streamflow, which aligns with the fundamental water balance. When cross-referenced against the Moran’s I analysis, we see that p_mean and p_seasonality are among the most highly spatially autocorrelated features. This suggests that while these variables are legitimate physical mechanisms, as precipitation is a direct input for runoff, they are likely also serving as geographic proxies for regional climate zones. In contrast, variables like pet_mean and root_depth_50 appear lower on the VIP list but possess much lower Moran’s I values, indicating they represent localized basin characteristics rather than broad geographic locations. The high importance of p_mean reflects a mix of mechanism and location; however, the model’s ability to maintain a spatial \(R^2\) of 0.743 confirms it is learning more than just regional relationships.

text:
Final fit & evaluate (25) — Week 6-1
Use the canonical tidymodels closing move (Week 6-1, The final fit!):

finalize_workflow() your tuned workflow with the best hyperparameters
Run last_fit() against your original initial_split() object — this trains on the full training set and evaluates on the held-out test set in one call
Use collect_metrics() and collect_predictions() to report final test-set performance
Create a plot of observed vs predicted logQmean on the test set with clear title, axis labels, and a compelling color scale (e.g., colored by aridity or region). Include a 1:1 reference line.
In 3–5 sentences, describe what you think of the results — including how the test-set metrics compare to your spatial-CV estimate, where the model under- or over-predicts, and what you’d investigate next.


# Finalize the workflow with the best mtry and min_n from tuning
final_wf <- rf_tune_wf %>%
  finalize_workflow(best_params)

# Perform the last fit on the initial split (75/25)
final_results <- last_fit(final_wf, camels_split)

# Collect final metrics and predictions

test_preds <- collect_predictions(final_results) %>%
  bind_cols(camels_test %>% select(aridity)) # Join aridity for coloring

    title = "Final Test Set Performance: Observed vs. Predicted logQmean",
    subtitle = "Coloring by aridity reveals potential systematic bias in dry vs. humid basins",
    x = "Observed log(Mean Streamflow)",

  ) +

Interpretation:
The final test-set results show that the model performs strongly overall, with predictions clustering tightly around the 1:1 line for humid basins (lower Aridity Index). However, as indicated by the Aridity Index coloring, the model exhibits a clear systematic bias by consistently over-predicting streamflow in highly arid basins, where points fall significantly below the dashed reference line. This performance closely mirrors the spatial-CV estimate, confirming that the model’s primary struggle is generalized hydrologic behavior in water-limited environments. To improve this, I would investigate the residuals of these high-aridity outliers specifically to see if adding predictors for ephemeral flow characteristics or subsurface storage could better capture the “losing stream” dynamics common in dry regions.
